read-history-with-log-and-show.label: Read History with log and show
read-history-with-log-and-show.summary: Scan the timeline with log, inspect one snapshot with show, and relate HEAD to the current project state.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Scan the One-Line History
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Read the three-entry history of `community-site/` in compact form.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git log --oneline compresses each snapshot to an identifier and subject so the project story is easy to scan.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Read the three-entry history of `community-site/` in compact form.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

main_sh.content:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf community-site mkdir -p community-site git -C community-site init -q -b main git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local" cd community-site printf %s '

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Start community site' cd community-site printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"]; ' > nav.js cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add events navigation' cd community-site printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon. ' > README.md cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Clarify volunteer hours'

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Start community site' cd community-site printf %s 'export const links = ["Home", "Events"]; ' > nav.js cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add events navigation' cd community-site printf %s 'Open Saturdays from 9 AM to noon. ' > README.md cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Clarify volunteer hours'

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Inspect the Previous Snapshot
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The newest commit changed the README. Inspect `HEAD~1` to review the snapshot immediately before it.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: HEAD~1 names the first parent of the current commit, which is the snapshot immediately before HEAD in this linear history.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.title: Connect History to Current State
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Read the one-line history, inspect the latest snapshot, and finish with short status to confirm that inspection changed nothing.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: History inspection is read-only: log and show explain snapshots without changing the working tree.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.starterCode:
# Use the terminal for this Git task.
# The required starting files and repository state are already prepared.
try_read_history_with_log_and_show_sketc.solutionCode:
git log --oneline
git show --stat HEAD
git status --short

single-choice-1.title: HEAD in Git
single-choice-1.prompt: What does `HEAD` refer to in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Think about the current state of your working directory.

help.concept: `HEAD` points to the current commit in your branch.
help.hint_1: It usually points to the latest commit in your branch.
help.hint_2: `HEAD` helps you understand the current state of your project.

options.a: The first commit in the repository
options.c: The latest commit in the current branch

multi-choice-1.title: Understanding git log --oneline
multi-choice-1.hint: Think about the format and details shown by this command.

help.concept: `git log --oneline` provides a brief overview of the commit history.
help.hint_1: It shows each commit on a single line.
help.hint_2: Each line includes a short hash and the commit message.

options.a: A detailed diff of each commit
options.b: A single line summary of each commit
options.c: The full commit message and author details

multi-choice-2.title: Purpose of git show
multi-choice-2.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git show` command?
multi-choice-2.hint: Consider what details this command reveals about a commit.

help.concept: `git show` provides detailed information about a specific commit.
help.hint_1: It shows the changes made in a commit.
help.hint_2: It includes the commit message, author, and date.

options.a: To list all commits in a branch
options.b: To show detailed information about a specific commit
options.c: To display the current branch name

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Git Commands
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the following Git commands in the order you would use them to inspect a commit's history and details.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Read each piece and arrange them in the order the statement should be understood.

help.concept: The pieces should form a valid statement in a logical order.
help.hint_1: Start with the piece that introduces the idea or action.
help.hint_2: Place dependent pieces after the part they describe or complete.

fill-blank-1.title: Command for concise commit history
fill-blank-1.prompt: Fill in the blank: The command `git [blank1] --oneline` provides a concise view of the commit history.
fill-blank-1.hint: This command is used to list commits briefly.

help.concept: `git the missing term --oneline` is used to view a brief commit history.
help.hint_1: The command starts with `git` and is followed by a word that means 'record'.
help.hint_2: The missing word is `the missing term`.

fill-blank-1.template: The command `git [blank1] --oneline` provides a concise view of the commit history.

fill-blank-2.title: Command to inspect a commit
fill-blank-2.prompt: Fill in the blank: Use `git [blank1] HEAD` to inspect the details of a specific commit.
fill-blank-2.hint: This command reveals detailed information about a commit.

help.concept: `git the missing term` is used to inspect a specific commit.
help.hint_1: The command starts with `git` and is followed by a word that means 'display'.
help.hint_2: The missing word is `the missing term`.

fill-blank-2.template: Use `git [blank1] HEAD` to inspect the details of a specific commit.

sketch0.title: Scan the Timeline
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
`git log --oneline` turns the commit history into a compact list:

```bash
git log --oneline
```

Each row pairs a shortened identifier with a subject. Newest appears first. The identifier is useful for selecting a commit, but the message and order are what make the history understandable.

Use the list to find the checkpoint you want to investigate.

sketch1.title: Open One Commit
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
`git show` opens a commit and displays its metadata and patch:

```bash
git show --stat --oneline HEAD
```

`HEAD` selects the current commit. `--stat` summarizes which files changed, while `--oneline` keeps the heading compact.

Remove `--stat` when you need the full line-by-line patch.

sketch2.title: Understand HEAD
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
HEAD is Git’s name for the commit currently checked out. On `main`, it normally points to the newest commit on that branch.

```bash
git rev-parse --short HEAD
```

The output is an identifier, not a version number you should memorize. As new commits are created, HEAD moves forward.

Thinking of HEAD as “the snapshot I am currently standing on” makes status and history easier to connect.

sketch3.title: Move Between Overview and Detail
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
History inspection works like a table of contents and a chapter. Start with `git log --oneline` for the overview, then use `git show` for the selected detail.

This is faster and safer than opening every file and guessing what changed. It also lets you verify whether a commit message accurately describes its snapshot.

A readable history supports both views without surprises.
